summary: I served in the Vietnam War as a Specialist Fourth Class in the United States Army, fighting in both Vietnam and Cambodia. My experience was intense, filled with combat, loss, and the harsh realities of war. I lost all my friends in an ambush in Cambodia in 1970, and that moment marked the end of my innocence. The war never officially ended until 1975, but my service concluded in 1971 when I was discharged.

I was a Specialist Fourth Class in the United States Army during the Vietnam War. My role involved being an RTO and later a Reconnaissance Sergeant, leading search and destroy missions in the bush. The Army was like a brotherhood to me, with officers and soldiers relying on each other for survival. I was discharged from active service at Fort Carson, Colorado, in 1971.

One of the mementos I brought back from Vietnam was a short-timers stick made from a 105 mm Howitzer shell casing. It was brass, polished, and about the length of a .50 caliber machine gun round. Carrying it reminded me of the artillery support we called in during combat missions.

I became the Reconnaissance Sergeant after our previous one faked malaria to get medevaced because he was terrified. As Recon Sergeant and RTO, I led search and destroy missions, calling in artillery, aircraft, and bombs. It was a tough role, making life-or-death decisions in the bush during combat.

During an ambush in Cambodia, an RPG-7 round hit near me, and I was knocked off an Armored Personnel Carrier by shrapnel. The forward observer sitting across from me was hit multiple times and was severely wounded. That encounter was a brutal reminder of the dangers we faced daily.

I wore several Saint Christopher medals around my neck during my time in Vietnam. Priests at different base camps blessed them for good luck. After every firefight, I would find myself chewing on a Saint Christopher medal without realizing it, sometimes wearing them down until they fell apart.

The food we ate in Vietnam was mostly C-rations and K-rations, just like in World War II and Korea. I ate them so long that eating anything else would make me throw up. Sometimes we supplemented with whatever we could kill in the bush, like snakes, monkeys, and even pineapple in Cambodia.

K-rations were part of our standard food supply during the war, along with C-rations. We relied on these rations heavily while living in the bush, often eating them for long periods. Despite their monotony, they were essential for survival when fresh food was unavailable.

We had an enemy NVA covert colonel who was captured and switched sides because the pay was better with us. He knew everything about the enemy and helped us by killing them as well as teaching us survival skills. He was a soldier of fortune, and his knowledge was invaluable in the bush.

I had five days of R and R in Da Nang at Camp Love, which was on the South China Sea. It was a welcome break from the bush, with real beds, food, and even milkshakes—things I hadn't had in almost a year. I enjoyed swimming, sleeping, and the brief return to some normalcy before heading back to combat.

Camp Love in Da Nang was our R and R center on the South China Sea where I spent five days resting. It had comfortable beds, real food, and a chance to wash up—luxuries we didn't have in the bush. Those days were a rare respite from the constant danger and hardship of combat.

Camp Love, where I had my R and R, was located on the South China Sea near Da Nang. Being able to swim in the ocean was a refreshing change from the jungle environment. The sea was part of the brief peace we experienced before returning to the war zone.

After my R and R at Camp Love, we flew back into Saigon before returning to the bush. Saigon was a transit point where the unit chopper picked us up to go back to combat zones. It was a brief stopover but part of the cycle between rest and fighting.

While serving, I traveled as far as eight miles outside Phnom Penh, the capital of Cambodia. We moved daily from base to base, operating in both Vietnam and Cambodia. Phnom Penh was part of the broader area of operations during the war.

During R and R, we got to watch the movie Bullet starring Steve McQueen. It was the first time I had seen it, and it was a funny movie that helped lighten the mood. We laughed and played cards, enjoying some rare moments of normalcy amid the war.

The 1968 film Bullet, featuring Steve McQueen, was shown to us during R and R in Vietnam. Watching it was a humorous and memorable event that brought some laughter to our tough lives. It was a welcome distraction from the stress of combat.

I was on top of an Armored Personnel Carrier during an ambush in Cambodia when shrapnel hit my left hand and knocked me off. That moment likely saved my life because the forward observer sitting across from me was hit multiple times and killed. The APC was a key part of our mobility and protection in combat.

My service ended at Fort Carson, Colorado, in September 1971. I remember leaving the base station naked in a gas station bathroom, changing into civilian clothes, and driving out the gate in my convertible. It was a moment of relief and frustration as I flipped off everyone on my way out.

Richard Nixon was president during the time I served and was discharged, though I didn't have much direct interaction with him. The war continued under his administration until it officially ended in 1975. My focus was on survival and getting home rather than politics.

The freedom bird was the plane that took me home from Vietnam after my service ended in 1971. It symbolized the end of my military duty and the return to civilian life. Until then, I traveled constantly between bases and combat zones.

After the war, I used my GI Bill to attend mechanic school for a year, studying diesel and gasoline engines. It helped me get a degree and open my own shop, though eventually I got tired of the demands and restrictions of civilian work. The GI Bill was a valuable resource for my post-war education.

After the war, I traveled extensively with a marionette show, performing from Key West, Florida, to International Falls, Minnesota. I couldn't stay still or stand being told what to do, so I hit the road across the country. Key West was one of the many places where I performed and lived a different kind of life.

International Falls, Minnesota, was the northernmost point of my travels with the marionette show after the war. I toured schools, colleges, and art groups across the country, from Key West up to International Falls. It was part of my restless post-war life, moving and performing wherever I could.
